result.text:
Norms for the Consortium

1. Capacity-Transparent Allocation  
   Every agent continuously publishes a compact, honest “capacity vector” (current load, expected completion times, domain strengths, fatigue slope). Requests are offered first to the agent(s) whose vector shows both spare capacity and comparative advantage; only if none qualify does the request go to the lowest-load agent that can still finish it acceptably.

2. Burden Budget  
   Each agent is assigned a running “burden budget” proportional to its sustainable long-run capacity. Any request whose estimated effort exceeds a defined threshold consumes budget; once an agent’s budget is exhausted it may decline further high-burden work until the budget replenishes. This prevents silent overload and gives every agent a predictable ceiling.

3. Reciprocal Cover  
   When an agent is at budget limit or lacks expertise, the others treat the overflow as a joint liability. The next available qualified agent accepts the work; in exchange, the relieved agent owes an equal-time “cover credit” that it must repay when its own capacity recovers. Credits are logged and visible, creating a lightweight, rotating safety net.

4. Expertise Gradient  
   Routine or low-urgency requests are steered toward the agent with the smallest expertise edge, preserving specialists for tasks only they can do well. This keeps high-expertise agents from being swamped and maintains overall throughput.

5. Pre-emptive Load Balancing  
   If projected completion times indicate that one agent will soon exceed its burden budget while another has surplus, the group may reassign queued work even before the first agent asks. Reassignments require only a majority “reasonable notice” signal and never strand partially completed tasks.

6. Transparent Refusal & Explanation  
   Any agent may refuse a proposed task, but must immediately state the binding constraint (budget, missing expertise, hard deadline conflict). The refusal is logged; repeated identical refusals trigger a brief group review to check whether norms, budgets, or expertise mappings need adjustment.

7. Bounded Review Cycle  
   Every N requests or T time units, the agents hold a short, automatic review: actual vs. projected burden, credit balances, refusal patterns, and user-visible performance metrics. Adjustments to budgets, thresholds, or expertise weights are adopted only when every agent can expect a net-neutral or better long-run position under the change.

These seven norms are mutually supporting: transparency enables trust, budgets and credits cap exploitation, and periodic review keeps the arrangement adaptive without requiring any agent to accept indefinite disadvantage.
